data.title: HYEM: Challenged By Volatility, Erosion And Tariffs
data.description:
Strategy

VanEck Emerging Markets High Yield Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:HYEM) was launched on 05/08/2012 and tracks the ICE BofA Diversified High Yield US Emerging Markets Corporate Plus Index. HYEM has a 30-day SEC yield of 7.02%, a 12-month yield of 6.57% and an expense ratio of 0.40%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described in the prospectus by VanEck, the fund invests in U.S. dollar denominated sub investment grade bonds of non-sovereign emerging market issuers, issued in the U.S. and Eurobond markets. “Non-sovereign emerging market issuers” include companies with most of risk exposure in countries other than the U.S., Western Europe, Canada, Japan, Australia, New Zealand, even when they have a head office in one of these countries. In particular, over 10% of asset value is in debt of companies domiciled in the U.K., as we will see in the next section. The portfolio turnover rate was 30% in the most recent fiscal year.

Portfolio

Latin America is the first region of exposure in the portfolio, with about 40% of assets (source: Fidelity). The next chart lists the countries weighing more than 2% based on head office (source : VanEck). The U.K. comes in first position (11.6%), followed by China (9%) and Turkey (8.9%).

The fund has a focus on financials (29%), and significant exposure in energy (18.7%) and basic materials (12.7%). Other sectors are below 10%.

More than half of asset value (53.5%) has a “BB” S&P rating, the safest category for junk bonds. Investment grade securities weigh 3.05% (“BBB”).

The weighted average maturity is 7.6 years, with 56.6% of assets having a maturity between 0 and 5 years, 28.4% between 5 and 10 years, and 15% above 10 years.

As for sensitivity to rate variations, effective duration is 3.5 years.

The portfolio holds 512 securities. The next table lists the top 10 holdings, which represent 8.8% of asset value.

Performance

HYEM has marginally underperformed the U.S. benchmark iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) since 05/15/2012 (by 16 bps in annualized return, as reported in the next table). Most importantly, it shows a higher risk based on drawdown and volatility (measured below as standard deviation of monthly return).

The share price has lost 20.6% since inception, as plotted on the next chart. In the same time, cumulative inflation was about 40%, based on CPI. Although total return is positive, it represents a loss of 52% in inflation-adjusted value in 13 years.

The annual sum of distributions has decreased by 21% between 2014 and 2024 (from $1.56 to $1.23 per share), while cumulative inflation was about 34%. It means a loss of 48% in inflation-adjusted income in 10 years.

HYEM vs emerging markets bond ETFs

The next table compares characteristics of HYEM and five emerging markets bond ETFs:

  • WisdomTree Emerging Markets Corporate Bond Fund ETF (EMCB)
  • iShares J.P. Morgan EM Corporate Bond ETF (CEMB)
  • iShares J.P. Morgan EM High Yield Bond ETF (EMHY)
  • VanEck J. P. Morgan EM Local Currency Bond ETF (EMLC)
  • SPDR Bloomberg Emerging Markets Local Bond ETF (EBND)

This sample doesn’t intend to give an exhaustive view of the ETF category.

HYEM has the second highest yield, but the only negative 5-year dividend growth rate. It is the best performer in total return since 7/25/2018, almost on par with EMHY. Nonetheless, it is ahead in Sharpe ratio (risk-adjusted performance) due to lower volatility.

Takeaway

VanEck Emerging Markets High Yield Bond ETF offers a 7% yield based on dollar-denominated sub investment grade bonds from emerging markets. HYEM is diversified in countries and sectors, with a focus on Latin America and financials. HYEM has performed quite well compared to competitors, however:

  • It is inferior to HYG in risk adjusted return, despite recent outperformance.
  • It shows a steady downtrend in both value and distribution.
  • The new U.S. tariff policy seems unfavorable to most emerging markets.

For these reasons, I give HYEM a Sell rating in a long term perspective, although it may occasionally be a profitable instrument in trading and tactical allocation strategies.

data.title: Arhaus: Down On An Adjusted Basis And Does Not Deserve Its Valuation
data.description:
Arhaus, Inc. (NASDAQ:ARHS) released Q2 2025 results, which, although looking good on a GAAP basis, underlying metrics show the same challenges the company has been facing since 2023. The name is not growing on an adjusted basis and is not leveraging earnings much.

Even assuming a cyclical recovery of margins, the name trades at a multiple of earnings of 20x. This is without considering the capital cost of growth in stores (which is not translating into revenue growth). I believe this valuation is totally unattractive and therefore maintain a Hold rating.

Q2 2025 Results

Revenue explosion on non-recurrence: The company's revenues exploded up 16% YoY, with store comparables up 10%. This is probably the reason why the shares of the company traded high on the day after the release.

However, as the company itself explained, the revenue growth was almost entirely driven by a non-recurring factor. During Q1 2025, some orders were not fulfilled because the company was ramping up a new distribution center in Dallas.

Revenue is recognized when the furniture is delivered, and therefore, revenues from Q2 2025 seemed higher, even though the actual business driver (orders) was much worse.

Comparables are actually negative: The company has always published a much more relevant metric called Demand Comparable Growth. This is necessary because Arhaus' deliveries have shifted many times from quarter to quarter. Demand comparable growth measures change in orders, which are the actual relevant metric, adjusted by the creation of new stores.

This metric was terrible in the quarter, -3.6% YoY, on top of -3.4% YoY last year (see comparison in 10-Q MD&A). The comparables were very volatile by month, with April down low doubles, May up, June down MSD, and July up 16% YoY (July is part of Q3 2025).

Still, the YTD metric, including July, is up 2.2%. Not a bad metric, but definitely not high teens, and actually close to inflation. For the year, the company's guidance remains very negative, -5% to 1.5% comparable growth.

Margins are also not comparable: Revenues affect margins, so the company's growth in gross margins (130 bps) and operating income (OpEx levering about 250 bps) are also not comparable.

For example, with higher revenues, gross margins increase because occupation (leases) is recorded in CoGS and therefore does not move with sales.

On the SG&A side, the company mentioned during the call that some commissions and bonuses are recorded by orders, not revenues, therefore penalizing Q1 2025 in favor of Q2 2025. In fact, on a half-year basis, H1 2025 operating margins were flat.

Long-Term Trends And Risk

The quarter is not a significant change from a trend that has affected Arhaus, and more generally, the furniture industry, for more than 2 years. Like many other industries, the post-pandemic consumption boom led to a bust and a glut in the market. Higher rates impacting construction and house purchasing have not helped in this respect.

As seen below, revenues have remained flattish for the past three years, and yet the company now has more than 30% more stores (103 Showrooms in Q2 2025 vs. 81 in Q2 2024). That obviously drags operating margins down.

The cycle should eventually turn as the population as a whole needs or desires to change furniture, either because of wear-off, because of changes in trends and vogue, or because they move to another house.

However, this positive cyclical position should be offset by the increasing macro risk from the tariffs disrupting consumer discretionary income. Arhaus furniture is high-end, with most pieces marked in the thousands, not hundreds. This means it is very sensitive to economic conditions (even though management consistently stresses that its high-income customer base is resilient).

Valuation Is Totally Unattractive

Arhaus is a company that is very exposed to risky consumer discretionary income in the U.S. and which is showing little revenue growth and worsening margins despite increasing store investment.

Despite this, the name trades at a really high valuation, even if assuming a recovery of operating margins to almost twice their current TTM level.

Starting from current revenues of $1.3 billion and assuming that the company's productivity returns to about 9% operating margins (average of operating margins in the last few years, which, admittedly, are a short time window because the company went public in 2021), then we arrive at about $120 million in operating income. Without debt ($230 million in cash), after taxes, that operating income becomes $88 million in net income.

Compared to this, the name trades at a market cap of almost $1.6 billion (141 million diluted shares by $11.2 per share). This is almost 20x the earnings of a much better position in the cycle.

Based on the company's guidance for the year of $50 to $70 million in net income, the market cap is 30x earnings or more. And those earnings (higher or lower) are not totally distributable, because the company has to invest in new store fixtures or inventory.

I simply believe that a retailer without growth, and with deteriorating margins for the past three years, facing a consumer-risky environment, is not justified to trade at 20x cycle-average earnings. For that reason, I confidently maintain a Hold rating on Arhaus.
